data.title: Italgas: Steady Income Growth Through Market Volatility
data.description:
Introduction

We heard the world was shrinking and everyone was coming closer. This April, we reversed our approach and began thinking locally. We are looking for companies with strong regional businesses and local suppliers that could shield them from what appears to be a trade war between several geopolitical powers in the world.

A company that doesn't seem to be directly affected by the tariffs the Trump administration recently announced is Italgas S.p.A. (OTCPK:ITGGF), the largest gas distributor in Europe.

With the core of its business happening in Italy and Greece, Italgas also plays a key role in the Mediterranean Sea and the gas hub role that Southern Europe is expected to play as Europe diversifies its gas suppliers from Russia.

As I have shown in my previous articles, Italgas operates in a strategic sector such as gas distribution, but has also expanded into the water sector and energy efficiency. Moreover, it is investing in the digitalization of its energy system. Finally, it is a leader in renewable gas solutions development (biomethane and hydrogen). Its "Power-to-Gas" project in Sardinia, for example, uses electricity from renewables to produce hydrogen through the process of electrolysis. The hydrogen that is then produced can be converted into methane or even stored for future usage.

Italgas' FY24 Review

Now, Italgas has been able to report a streak of 30+ consecutive quarters of growth. This proves the resilience and the low volatility of its operations. In particular, the company has been able to increase its margins, and in FY it reported its first-ever year with over EUR 1B in FCF.

Italgas closed its FY2024 with revenues close to EUR 1.8B, with a 14% YoY EBITDA increase to EUR 1.4B and a 20.5% surge in its reported EBIT of EUR 821M.

Its balance sheet is quite solid. The company spends on capex around EUR 900M per year, showcasing ongoing digitization and network enhancement efforts. The net financial debt stood at EUR 6.8B, more or less flat YoY. The average cost of debt increased by 30bps YoY to 1.6%. Yes, it is not a typo, and it shows how convenient it can be to invest in Italgas.

Italgas' Dividend

The strong free cash flow generation enabled Italgas to further improve its dividend policy. As of December 31, 2023, Italgas committed to distributing dividends with a guaranteed minimum 4% growth vs. 2022. Well, now the minimum hike is 5% for 2024 vs. 2023. But the dividend policy also aims at a 65% payout. Italgas will pay the higher of the two possibilities, that is, a 5% hike is guaranteed, but it could also be higher. For example, this upcoming May, Italgas will pay a DPS of EUR 0.406, which is a 15.3% YoY growth.

This dividend policy currently makes the stock yield over 6% (keep in mind the Italian withholding tax is 26%), and offers steady income. This is why Italgas is not selling off big this month, although the recent weakness in the markets is bringing the stock down, making it more alluring.

We should also be aware that the company is moving ahead with a strategic plan through 2030 that should double its top line. At the same time, the expected EBITDA should reach EUR 2.8B, which is a bit over 2x Italgas' 2024 EBITDA. With this expansion of its revenues and profits, the company's leverage should stay between 62% and 65%, with its net debt growing in the teens. This shows that Italgas manages its balance sheet with caution, but can also address a brisk growth pace.

Therefore, I think we can reasonably expect the dividend to grow at a faster pace than the minimum threshold (a 5% growth through 2030 would give a DPS of EUR 0.544, an 8.5% yield based on today's stock price in Milan hovering around EUR 6.40). I think we would not be too bold to expect the company to report at least EUR 1B in net income in 2030. Assuming the share count stays the same at 811M, we could have a 2030 DPS of EUR 1.20. Let's say this is too optimistic, we could expect the DPS to be around EUR 0.87, which is the average of the two hypotheses we discussed. The fwd yield would be 13.6%. Not bad at all, especially considering there is no sign of Italgas being a value trap.

Valuation

Currently, Italgas trades at a P/E ratio of around 10 and has a market cap of EUR 5.4B. Sometimes, the stock dips to below EUR 5.00 per share, but recently, Italgas has attracted new investors thanks to its reliable dividend policy. In April, it gave up only 6% of its stock price, which was heading north toward EUR 7.00. Assuming FY25 net profits of EUR 620M (around a 15% YoY increase), we would have Italgas trading at a fwd PE just below 9x. I think this valuation is reasonable, especially for a defensive stock that is poised to keep generating income through market volatility. Moreover, its network effects linked to its leading position in Southern Europe should help it capitalize even further on the trend that sees Europe looking at Africa and the Middle East for its gas supplies. As a result, I continue to rate Italgas as a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AbbVie: Thriving Beyond Humira's Patent Cliff (Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

AbbVie Inc. (NYSE:ABBV) stock is up 16% compared to S&P 500 returns of 9.41% since my last look in November 2023. I evaluated AbbVie after its $10 billion acquisition of cancer developer, ImmunoGen. ImmunoGen’s flagship drug, Elahere, was a first-in-class antibody-drug conjugate, or ADC, approved for platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. Its pipeline included other ADCs. The acquisition came when AbbVie was facing intense competition for its blockbuster drug, Humira, after its loss of exclusivity (LOE). The giant pharmaceutical company has kept its pockets open. Just a month after acquiring ImmunoGen, AbbVie splurged on Cerevel Therapeutics and its neuroscience-focused pipeline (including emraclidine and tavapadon for Alzheimer’s disease psychosis and Parkinson’s disease, respectively). The $8.7 billion deal was completed last August.

While AbbVie remains engaged in legal battles concerning Humira biosimilars, Humira revenues continue to decline. At its peak, Humira was generating $21 billion (2022). So, it was easily one of the best-selling drugs in all history. Biosimilars entered the market in 2023, causing price and market share erosion. In 2024, Humira product revenues dropped ~38% to reach just $8.993 billion. However, judging by its stock performance and growth in other key products, AbbVie has done a solid job making up for Humira’s LOE.

Rising Immunology Stars: Skyrizi and Rinvoq

Other immunology drugs, like Skyrizi and Rinvoq, have blossomed into monster drugs. Their growth has completely made up for Humira’s loss. Skyrizi (plaque psoriasis, Crohn’s, and ulcerative colitis) revenues jumped 50.9% year-over-year to reach $11.718 billion in 2024. Likewise, Rinvoq (arthritis, atopic dermatitis) grew 50.5% year-over-year to reach $5.971 billion.

AbbVie is expanding Rinvoq’s label to include Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA). Just yesterday, the company announced that the European Commission approved Rinvoq, making it the only JAK inhibitor approved in the E.U. Current GCA treatment recommendations call for steroids, so Rinvoq's approval could alter the treatment landscape. Relative to other immunology indications, GCA is a much smaller market opportunity, coming in at under $1 billion.

Impressively, AbbVie projects that Rinvoq and Skyrizi, together, can achieve over $31 billion in revenue in 2027. This represents a CAGR of approximately 21% and a raise of $4 billion from its last projection.

Oncology Portfolio Evolution

AbbVie’s oncology segment features Imbruvica, a first-generation Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor used predominantly for liquid tumors. This drug is now facing competition from second-generation BTK inhibitors like AstraZeneca PLC's (AZN) Calquence (which saw 25% year-over-year growth in 2024). So, although the overall market for BTK inhibitors is large and growing, AbbVie’s Imbruvica is slowly losing market share. Indeed, $3.347 billion in revenues for Imbruvica represented a 6.9% year-over-over decline in 2024.

Thankfully, steady growth from Venclexta (up 12.9% to reach $2.583 billion) and a robust launch from Elahere have made up for Imbruvica. The latter achieved $477 million in 2024, which was a partial year. Together, AbbVie’s oncology portfolio grew an impressive 10.8%.

Neuroscience and Aesthetics: Additional Growth Pillars

AbbVie’s neuroscience efforts are paying off and seeing robust growth (up 16.6% year-over-year) thanks particularly to Botox Therapeutic (migraines, overactive bladder, spasticity) and Vraylar (schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depressive disorder adjunct).

AbbVie’s aesthetics portfolio, which features Botox Cosmetic and Juvederm, held steady (-2.2% year-over-year) despite fierce biosimilar competition in this market. Moreover, this segment is probably more vulnerable than others to macroeconomic headwinds. People spend less on aesthetics during difficult economic times. Despite this, AbbVie still envisions a “high single-digit CAGR for aesthetics through 2029.”

Earnings

Taking a closer look at Q4 earnings, revenues totaled $15.1 billion (up 6.1% year-over-year). Due to a $3.5 billion after-tax partial impairment charge related to emraclidine after it failed two Phase 2 schizophrenia trials, AbbVie’s GAAP diluted loss per share was $0.02. This specifically highlights the difficulty in neuroscience drug development and the inherent risks associated with acquiring clinical-stage biotechnology companies.

Earlier this week, AbbVie adjusted its guidance for full year 2025 and Q1 2025 as follows:

Financial Health

As of December 31, AbbVie held $5.52 billion in cash and equivalents. Total current assets were $25.58 billion, while total current liabilities were $38.75 billion. Subsequently, AbbVie’s current ratio is under 1.0, which is not ideal. Long-term debt and finance lease obligations totaled $60.34 billion.

AbbVie consistently generates between $18 billion and $22 billion in cash from operations each year.

Due to robust cash generation, it can afford some wiggle room when it comes to short-term liquidity and leverage.

Valuation Assumptions

Upon discounted cash flow analysis, there is a slight disconnect between what growth expectations are implied by AbbVie’s intrinsic value and what analysts project.

Assuming just 3% revenue growth (on average over my nine-year projection period) and free cash flow (FCF) margins of 31% (where it has been historically), AbbVie appears undervalued by ~10%.

My sensitivity analysis reveals there is a good amount of margin of safety in the event key assumptions (i.e., growth and margin) change.

Additionally, there is an asymmetric upside (2.20x) if AbbVie exceeds baseline expectations (i.e., 3% revenue growth).

My Monte Carlo simulation accounts for variability among key inputs like growth, margins, beta, and cost of debt. So, it provides a more realistic and dynamic view of possible share price outcomes.

The upside-to-downside ratio (% change from current price to 95th and 5th percentile share prices) of 3.1:1 implies a favorable risk/reward profile. This also tells us that while AbbVie may be undervalued, its valuation is within reason. Overall, there is a better chance for an upside than there is a downside.

Conclusion

Despite some hiccups (e.g., Phase 2 outcomes for the Cerevel assets, near-term EPS pressure related to acquired IPR&D, and asset impairments from M&A activity), AbbVie continues to see robust growth in key segments. For many companies, Humira’s LOE would have been difficult to offset. Through consistent innovation and leadership in immunology (e.g., Skyrizi and Rinvoq) and strategic expansion into oncology and neuroscience, AbbVie has navigated Humira’s LOE gracefully.

There are some risks to consider. AbbVie’s aggressive M&A has resulted in a substantial debt burden, which could reduce financial flexibility. Humira’s revenue erosion could accelerate faster than expected. Its aesthetics portfolio, which declined in 2024, could be vulnerable to macroeconomic pressures on discretionary spending or increasing competition.

Overall, AbbVie's outlook leans positive. The stock appears a bit undervalued per DCF modeling and also offers an attractive 3.73% dividend yield. Its robust cash generation enables the advancement of key pipeline assets, creating a flywheel effect that cannot easily be replicated (as seen with drugs like Skyrizi replacing the revenue losses from Humira). AbbVie is a Buy and can be a solid addition to any diversified portfolio.

data.title: RBC Bearings: A True Quality Stock At A Demanding Valuation
data.description:
In the summer of 2023, I believed that RBC Bearings Incorporated (NYSE:RBC) was rolling on. The producer of high-quality niche products was moving along just fine, and fast forwarding to today the business has seen solid (earnings) growth, being awarded more lofty valuations with the passage of time.

This means that expectations have risen quite a bit and while RBC stock undoubtedly is of great quality, I fear that current prices reflect a bit too much of that quality here.

A Specialized Product Business

RBC Bearings is a producer of highly engineered precision bearings, components, and essential systems which are used in industrial, aerospace and defense industries. The business has only been around 34 years, and has been very successfully led by CEO Michael Hartnett.

The company has been publicly listed since 2005, as it has seen impressive growth thanks to an impressive acquisition streak which totals nearly 30 transactions.

As of today, the company generates about $1.6 billion in sales and is largely a domestic business, with nearly 90% of sales generated in the U.S. About two-thirds of sales are generated from industrial applications, complemented by the aerospace & defense business. The business is split 50/50 even between OEM and distribution/aftermarket services.

Bearings are key, used in various machines, aircraft and systems as these products allow for motion, reduced wear to moving parts, while reducing damage and energy loss at large. Key OEMs, which use its products, include names like Caterpillar, Boeing, Airbus, and other big related names.

Over the past three decades, the company has grown sales at an impressive CAGR around 11% per annum. All this has translated into remarkable returns for investors, as a $15 stock at the time of the public offering in 2005 has risen to levels in the $300s here.

This comes as investors over time have digested a bit of dilution as well, largely resulting from inorganic growth efforts made by the company, including a huge $2.9 billion deal to acquire Doge. This deal, which was announced in 2021, involved the purchase of the mechanical power transmission unit from ABB after the pandemic.

The deal resulted in high leverage ratios around 4 times, as the acquisition only contributed in full to the 2023 results, with revenues up 55% to $1.47 billion that year, with organic growth reported at 11%. Adjusted earnings were reported at $218 million, equal to $7.48 per share. Net debt was down to $1.3 billion already, for a 3.0 times leverage ratio.

With the business having digested the deal well, the company was on track to generate about $1.6 billion in sales, with earnings seen up around $8 per share. Trading at $217 in August 2023 shares were not cheap at 24-27 times earnings, assuming that earnings would come in between $8-$9 per share, while there still was some leverage apparent on the balance sheet.

Moving Steady

Since the summer of 2023, shares have gradually made further inroads as they rose to absolute highs of a $370 in the fall of last year, now having fallen back to the $305 mark, amidst the turmoil caused by the new tariffs and perhaps full-blown trade war.

Early in 2024, the company posted its fiscal 2024 results, a year in which revenues were up 6% to $1.56 billion. Adjusted earnings were up about a dollar to $8.62 per share. About $17 million of the reconciliation, or about half a dollar after-tax, was adjusted in relation to stock-based compensation expenses, which made realistic earnings trended around $8 per share.

Net debt was down to $1.13 billion and with adjusted EBITDA improving to $482 million, it was the combination of absolute net debt being reduced and improved earnings, which reduced leverage ratios all the way to 2.3 times. The company guided for a 7-8% increase in first quarter sales for the fiscal year 2025, but failed to provide a full-year guidance.

What Now?

The results released so far for the fiscal 2025 were not too inspiring, with revenues up 4% and change through the first nine months to $1.20 billion. This was due to industrial sales which came in flattish, in fact, were down slightly on the year, with aerospace & defense sales up in a spectacular fashion.

GAAP earnings rose in a pronounced fashion, aided by the elimination of preferred stock (and its dividends) as adjusted earnings so far this year were up a dollar to $7.16 per share, coming in a long way to exceed $9.50 per share, although that the adjustment for stock-based compensation is a bit on the rise, making me work with a realistic earnings numbers just short of $9 per share.

Net debt is down to $940 million and with this metric coming in at $380 million over the past nine months, I peg leverage ratios at just 1.8 times, after the company has swiftly deleveraged following the deal with ABB, while it has been quiet on the dealmaking front since that larger deal.

Right now, shares trade at around 33 times earnings, if they are realistic at $9 per share, which is not very cheap even as the business shows 5% organic growth and leverage has come down a great deal. This comes as the earnings yield has compressed, amidst the sound performance and deleveraging attempts.

Even if shares are down 20% from the highs, shares are far from cheap here, also recognizing that shares only broke through the $300 mark in November of last year. While it can be argued that the company has a great aerospace and defense business, the counterargument is that industrial sales come in flattish, making a sum-of-the-part analysis likely not valid as well.

A Final Thought

Right now, I am erring cautious, given the relative outperformance of the business. I generally like the business, not just based on the performance in recent years, but really the fundamentals of the business and track record over the past decades.

While RBC Bearings Incorporated is arguably of great quality, the issue is that the business is awarded lofty valuations here at just a 3% earnings yield. This could be justified by the strong performance and leverage having come down.

Weighing it all together, a great business has been awarded rising valuation multiples over time, which makes me cautious here. I look forward to updating the thesis upon a period of stagnation or share pullbacks here.

data.title: Sage Therapeutics: A Speculative Play That Needs To Wait For A Turnaround Story
data.description:
Sage Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SAGE) used to be a ~$5bn company, the first time I covered it some four years ago. Today, it is a poor reflection of its former self, having lost 90% of its value. But there are certain attractions to SAGE.

Background

Sage, founded in 2010 to develop medicines for neuropsychiatric disorders, saw success in 2019 when the FDA approved ZULRESSO® (brexanolone) injection, making it the first treatment specifically indicated for postpartum depression (PPD). The next year, Sage signed a major collaboration deal with Biogen worth “$875 million and a $650 million equity investment as well as potential milestone payments, profit sharing and royalties.” The deal involved the development of Sage’s next-generation PPD molecule, zuranolone, and SAGE-324.

The approval, and this major deal, catapulted SAGE to the multi-billion-dollar market cap club. The winning streak continued until August 2023, when the FDA approved Zurzuvae (zuranolone) for PPD as the first oral treatment. However, along with that winner, the FDA also gifted them a CRL, rejecting their NDA for zuranolone in Major Depressive Disorder. Sage had never again reached the heights it had reached in 2019, with the first approval. The stock had broadly declined, but was still somewhat strong. Its downfall started with the MDD CRL, which, strangely, was the same time its second approval had come along.

The stock has been steadily falling since about mid-2023. In April 2024, while zuranolone had some decent revenue, another important molecule, dalzanemdor (SAGE-718) in Parkinson's disease did not meet its primary endpoint. Three months later, in July 2024, SAGE-324, another molecule with a partnership with Biogen, failed to meet the primary endpoint in a phase 2 study in essential tremor. By October, Sage decided to discontinue pursuing the MDD indication. By December, they discontinued ZULRESSO, because of declining revenue, a more difficult administration because Zurzuvae (zuranolone) had arrived, and also because they needed to save some money to continue as a company. The route was complete.

Current events

The dalzanemdor (SAGE-718) asset was a complete failure, failing the Parkinson’s program, and then going on to fail two other programs—mild cognitive impairment and mild dementia in Alzheimer's disease, and Huntington’s disease, where it had initially shown some success. Sage has now discontinued this molecule.

There is still some play left in Sage. It still has Zurzuvae (zuranolone) approved in the PPD indication. RBC Capital Market last year said they still see a $350mn-$400mn opportunity for Zurzuvae, which still had Biogen as its partner—meaning Sage doesn’t get all of that money. Thus, for Q3 2024:

The company’s take from sales of the drug Zurzuvae, also known as zuranolone, was $11M, representing a 49% increase from the prior quarter. Sage (SAGE) gets 50% of the net revenue generated by the drug, which is co-marketed with Biogen (BIIB).

However, as other analysts have noted, the molecule is “unlikely to generate near-term upside despite its long-term prospects.” Thus, Sage was in a dire position, and by late 2024, it was cutting costs and restructuring, just to remain a going concern.

An interesting offer came from Biogen on January 11, 2025, when it offered to buy out the company for around $469mn. Biogen owned 10.2% of Sage at that time. The stock was trading at around $5.55, and the offer price of $7.22 was a 30% premium to the price. Following the offer, SAGE stock surged 30% (of course). However, by the end of January, Sage management had rejected the offer, and probably rightly so.

Through this offer, Biogen was trying to fish in troubled waters. In the September quarter, Sage had a cash balance of $569mn (this was the data available to the public during the offer). By December, they had a cash balance of $504mn. This was more than the company’s market cap, but this was also more than the company’s cash balance. Yes, they did have around $82mn in long-term liabilities, however, even then, the offer “significantly undervalues” the company.

Sage has also sued Biogen over enforcement of a standstill agreement that they had since the beginning of their partnership. The agreement prohibited Biogen from acquiring additional Sage securities or proposing mergers under specific conditions. The company went on to say that management sees “blockbuster-plus” potential for Zurzuvae in postpartum depression and is happy with the current launch trajectory,” according to Mizuho analyst Uy Ear who spoke to management.

Zurzuvae’s potential and revenue

PPD occurs in between 1 in 10 and 1 in 7 women after giving birth. This translates to around 600,000 American women with the condition. Globally, there are hundreds of millions of women potentially facing this condition—“tens if not hundreds of millions annually if all countries are accounted for.” Antidepressants like Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs) are most commonly prescribed for PPD. Zurzuvae is the only approved drug. Its mechanism of action was described by Yale Medicine as follows:

Zuranolone is a synthetic form of allopregnanolone, a naturally occurring neurosteroid that the body makes from the hormone progesterone. Allopregnanolone helps the GABA-A receptors, which regulate mood and behavior, work a little better, Dr. Furey explains.

“And that leads to calming, antidepressant properties,” Dr. Furey says. “When a woman has postpartum depression, she doesn’t have enough allopregnanolone and her GABA-A receptors aren’t firing right, which is what makes her irritable, anxious, and depressed, among other symptoms,” Dr. Furey says.

As to efficacy, the Practice Advisory of the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists notes:

Zuranolone efficacy has been demonstrated in two phase 3 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter studies 78. The primary endpoint of both studies was the change in depressive symptoms using the total score from the 17-item Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAMD-17), measured at day 15. In both studies, patients in the zuranolone groups showed significantly more improvement in their symptoms compared to those in the placebo groups. The treatment effect was maintained at day 45—four weeks after the last dose of zuranolone.

One major problem with zuranolone is that its effect passes on to the fetus, or may pass through breast milk to the baby:

Available data from a clinical lactation study in 14 women indicate that zuranolone is present in low levels in human milk (see Data). There are no data on the effects of zuranolone on a breastfed infant and limited data on the effects on milk production.

Thus, without adequate data, it is not advisable for women taking zuranolone to nurse. Now, many medicines have these effects; however, since zuranolone is especially used by postnatal mothers, this is a key difficulty. However, being the only available therapy approved by the FDA, workarounds have to be taken.

The problem with the update of the drug is the slow recognition of PPD as a disorder. Here’s some discussion on that:

Mental health professionals have relied on a guidebook called the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, or DSM, to diagnose and treat their patients for more than 70 years.

But the DSM didn’t recognize PPD until the 1990s, when its fourth edition codified the condition as a “major depressive disorder occurring within four weeks of giving birth.”

The next edition, in 2013, went a bit further, defining PPD as a major depressive episode occurring during pregnancy or within four weeks after giving birth. The most recent update also highlights possible coexisting symptoms of anxiety and panic.

“Slowly the field grew so that eventually there actually was a formal diagnosis of depression,” said Wisner.

Despite all that, Zurzuvae’s revenues have grown. It had a list price of $15,900, less than half of Zulresso’s $34,000, and as an oral pill with a quick onset, it was easier, and better. The drug saw a strong first full year on the market in 2024, generating $36.1 million in collaboration revenue for Sage. Since this represented 50% of Biogen’s net Zurzuvae sales, total net product sales must have been approximately $72 million.

Nearly 6,600 prescriptions were shipped over the year, including 2,500 in the fourth quarter alone—this is a 21% increase from the third quarter. Payer coverage was robust, with over 95% of Commercial and Medicaid lives covered and all three major PBMs offering favorable policies.

Approximately 80% of prescriptions came from OB-GYNs, with a 60% repeat prescribing rate among targeted physicians and more than 70% of patients receiving ZURZUVAE as their first new treatment for postpartum depression. Aided brand awareness exceeded 90% among OB-GYNs and psychiatrists, and prescription growth in territories with recent sales force expansion reached 33% in Q4.

As of today, there is very little in the way of pipeline competition for zuranolone. The principle hurdle for the drug, besides the use of off-label antidepressants with questionable prognostic value, is the low recognition of PPD as a treatable disorder.

Financials

SAGE has a market cap of $409mn and a cash balance of $504mn. R&D and G&A expenses for the prior quarter were $37mn and $54mn, respectively. Their CFO for the last quarter was ($66.6mn), and with no capex, that gave them an FCF of -$66.6mn and a cash runway of 7–8 quarters. For a small company with a ruined pipeline, their OPEX is a bit on the higher side.

Risks

SAGE has gone from being a major innovative player in the CNS market to one that has failed multiple trials. Its current major risk is the update of Zurzuvae given the constraints I mentioned, as well as the near-absolute lack of a proper pipeline. It has a decent cash balance but given its high OPEX, its cash runway is modest.

Bottom line

SAGE remains a risky proposition for most retail investments. There are ways someone could take control of this company and turn it around—but that is not something retail could do. Therefore, despite its certain attractions, I will remain on the sidelines until someone who can change this company comes along.

data.title: Village Super Market: Shares Offer Additional Upside From Here
data.description:
Historically speaking, I have not been a big fan of the retail space. But every so often, I find it really attractive prospect in this industry. One firm that operates as a retailer in the food space that I have been consistently bullish about is Village Super Market, Inc. (NASDAQ:VLGEA). Since I last reaffirmed the company as a "buy" candidate back in October of last year, shares have skyrocketed by 22.5%. That compares to the 13.5% decline that the S&P 500 (SP500) saw over the same window of time. Going back a bit earlier, since I reaffirmed the company as a "buy" candidate in December 2021, shares have risen by 82.5%. The market is up only 11.6% over that timeframe.

Despite these significant moves higher, the stock still looks attractively priced in my book. This is true on both an absolute basis and relative to similar companies. Furthermore, despite the uncertain times that we are faced with in the economy, the business continues to grow, even though its physical footprint remains virtually unchanged. This positive expansion certainly deserves optimism as well. While I acknowledge that, compared to other similar firms, asset quality might not be the best, this is not enough to justify the huge gulf that exists between it and its peers from a valuation standpoint. Because of this, I've decided to keep the company rated a "buy" for now.

Shopping at Village Super Market

Village Super Market has a long history, dating way back to 1937. You would think that, with this old age, the enterprise would be significant in size. But that's not exactly the case. The firm has 34 supermarkets spread throughout New Jersey, New York, Maryland, and Pennsylvania. The largest chunk of these would be the 26th that it has in New Jersey. They fall under the ShopRite and Fairway brand names. The firm also has three Gourmet Garage specialty markets located in New York City. The business is interesting, in part, because it actually is part of a retailer owned food cooperative. That cooperative is called Wakefern Food Corporation and, historically, Village Super Market has been the second largest member of it.

Despite its small physical footprint, Village Super Market has seen some consistent and attractive growth as of late. In my last article about the company, I was able to cover financial results for the three years ending at the end of 2024. We now have figures covering through the second quarter of 2025. During the most recent quarter, the business reported revenue of $599.7 million. That translated to an increase of 4.2% compared to the $575.6 million reported the previous year.

Management attributed this to two things. First, comparable store sales grew by 2.3%. That's a slight improvement over the 2.2% rise achieved the previous year. Digital sales growth, growing pharmacy sales, stronger sales associated with stores that the firm had recently remodeled, and inflation in both its meat and dairy products ended up being responsible for this improvement. The business also saw some benefit from the opening of a replacement store in New Jersey. This occurred in March 2024.

Rising revenue, especially when it is accompanied by comparable store sales improvements, ends up resulting in a rise in profitability in most cases. And that is precisely what we saw here. During the most recent quarter, net income totaled $16.9 million. That's up from the $14.5 million reported the previous year. While the revenue increase was the big driver of this, the company did also see a reduction in costs in some areas. Most notably, Village Super Market saw its operating and administrative costs decline from 23.71% of sales to 23.22%. I know that, at first glance, this may not seem like much. But when applied to the revenue generated in just the most recent quarter, that's an extra $2.9 million in benefit accruing to the company's bottom line on a pre-tax basis.

Other profitability metrics ended up growing as well. Operating cash flow popped from $30.3 million to $40.8 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $24.4 million to $27 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $27.4 million to $31.3 million.

For the first half of 2025 as a whole, the picture for the business continued to improve. Revenue rose from $1.12 billion to $1.16 billion. Obviously, the opening of the aforementioned replacement store played a role in this. However, it is important to note that comparable store sales jumped 4.1%. The same exact factors that helped in the second quarter of 2025 alone helped for the first half of the year in its entirety.

Net income for the business also grew during this time. The rise in revenue played a larger portion of the increase from $26.1 million to $29.7 million. However, the most notable margin improvement involved its cost of sales, which contracted from 71.55% of revenue to 71.32%. This included benefits from lower warehouse assessment charges, higher patronage dividends and rebates, reduced LIFO inventory charges, and more.

Because of this, other profitability metrics improved also. Operating cash flow jumped from $48.7 million to $61 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a more modest increase from $46.1 million to $49.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $78.4 million to $86.5 million.

We don't really know what to expect when it comes to the rest of the year. Unfortunately, management hasn't really provided any details. If we annualize the results that we have seen so far, we would expect net profits for 2025 of $57.5 million. Adjusted operating cash flow would be $99.4 million, while EBITDA would be $107.7 million. Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. This chart also values the company using historical figures for 2024. No matter how you look at it, this is a business that, to me at least, looks attractive on an absolute basis.

Relative to other similar companies, Village Super Market also looks cheap. In the chart above, I compared it to five such firms. On both a price to earnings basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis, it ended up being the cheapest of the group. And when using the price to operating cash flow basis, we can see that one of the five companies is cheaper than it is. The business is also significantly cheaper than the competition on a price to book basis. In the chart below, you can see exactly what I mean. In fact, if it were to trade at the same price to book multiple as the cheapest of the five companies I compared it to, this would mean upside for shareholders of 224.4%.

Of course, we need to be cognizant of the fact that there can be differences in terms of overall operational quality. As part of my analysis into the company, I decided to look at the metrics that, I believe, are the most important. First and foremost, in the first chart below, you can see the return on equity for our candidate stacked up against using trailing 12-month data. Unfortunately, Village Super Market is worse off than any of the five companies I compared it to:

  • Natural Grocers by Vitamin Cottage, Inc. (NGVC)
  • The Kroger Co. (KR)
  • Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc. (SFM)
  • Casey's General Stores, Inc. (CASY)
  • Albertsons Companies, Inc. (ACI).

This is certainly a knock against the business.

In the subsequent chart, I repeated this analysis using return on assets. In this case, the picture is a bit better, with two of the five companies worse off than Village Super Market is. But still, that's not a great showing. Helping to offset this is the fact that our candidate does seem to have a really strong balance sheet. At the end of the most recent quarter, the firm had cash in excess of debt totaling $50.2 million. For a business with a market capitalization of $560.5 million, I would consider this to be a solid showing.

Takeaway

At this point in time, it seems to me as though Village Super Market is still very much undervalued. The business has a surplus of cash on its books and is growing nicely. Honestly, I would really like it if management would allocate additional capital toward growing the company's footprint. But by this point, I know I'm barking up the wrong tree. Some of the discount compared to comparable firms is certainly because of the company’s inability or unwillingness to grow its physical footprint. Some of it can also be attributed to the difference in asset quality between it and its peers. But even factoring those in, I believe that shares are far too cheap to justify anything other than a "buy" rating.

data.title: Voya Target Retirement Trust Series Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy Overview

This suite of Target Date Collective Trusts diversifies across asset classes and managers, and automatically adjusts the portfolio over time to help participants reach their retirement goals.

Market Review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald Trump’s presidential victory. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation, increased deficit spending and the Fed’s more conservative rate-cut path, as the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 bp, ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

The U.S. election, marked by Trump’s proposed tariffs, increased global uncertainty, led to further U.S. dollar strength and broadly weighed on international equities. Europe struggled with political instability in France and Germany and rising energy costs ahead of winter. In yen terms, Japanese equities rose due to strong corporate activity including strategic mergers, share buybacks and improved return on equity. Meanwhile, China dealt with weak consumer activity, property sector challenges and concerns about regulatory changes and economic restructuring. These combined factors caused international equities to continue to underperform.

Outlook

Global challenges and opportunities confront the markets in 2025. However, the U.S. remains attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation, and energy independence. Despite some softening, the U.S. labor market remains resilient. Inflation is expected to stay manageable, allowing the Fed to continue its gradual easing.

Supported by this positive outlook and President Trump’s pro-business proposals, we continue to overweight U.S. large and mid-cap stocks. Large-cap valuations appear expensive, but they are supported by strong corporate profitability. The increasing weight of the technology sector with its capital light business models suggest that higher margins may be the new norm, making expectations more reasonable. With the election concluded, corporate spending is expected to resume and drive positive earnings growth as well. Mid-caps offer enticing valuations and are poised to benefit from a broadening expansion and lower borrowing costs and provide more financial stability than small caps.

Outside the U.S., pockets of weakness are becoming more apparent, particularly in the eurozone and China. The eurozone faces cooling services and ongoing manufacturing challenges, heightening economic uncertainty. In China, new policies aim to address economic sluggishness, especially in the property market, and boost investor confidence. However, the investment environment looks more promising in Japan, where deflation has been curbed and corporate reforms have made progress. Despite this, there has been relatively little foreign investment, creating an opportunity as more investors recognize the growth story. Given this mixed picture, we continue to prefer U.S. equities.

In fixed income, U.S. IG core bonds offer decent yields and portfolio stability. While conviction on rates near-term direction is limited, we believe core bonds will hedge against equity risk due to the transition from inflation to growth risk that has occurred. While credit stress and defaults are expected to remain controlled, high yield spreads are very tight, leading us to prefer equities for risk allocation. In addition, an expectation that the U.S. dollar rally will continue and a positive U.S. economic outlook justify a preference for U.S. assets and an underweight in foreign currency denominated bonds.

Positioning

At the beginning of the period, Trusts were tactically overweight to U.S. mid-cap stocks and underweight in U.S. large-cap stocks. Emerging market (EM) equities were increased to remove an underweight by selling U.S. large cap and small cap. China’s recent enactment of monetary stimulus and its commitment to future fiscal measures indicate that policymakers are prepared to take action to meet their economic targets, which could provide a tailwind for EM stocks. In December, U.S. large-cap equities were reduced, and short-term bonds were increased. This move came after the post-election run-up and the elevated valuations, as we deemed it prudent to lock in some gains and diversify away from the momentum trade. In terms of manager changes, the BlackRock US Debt Index CIT was removed and proceeds were reallocated to Voya Core Plus CIT.

Overall, Trusts continue to maintain a balanced posture with a preference for U.S. large and mid-cap equities, and core IG fixed income.

Performance

The Voya Target Retirement Trusts’ primary performance objective is to outperform its strategic allocation composite benchmark over the long term through tactical asset allocation, i.e., deviating from the composite benchmark over the short and medium-term and active manager selection. The benchmark return is the weighted average return of indices that represent asset classes included in the strategic allocation benchmark. Index returns are gross of all fees. The Trusts are generally rebalanced monthly, and the strategic asset allocations are updated annually to reflect changes to our capital market assumptions. In the fourth quarter of 2024, Trusts’ outperformed their strategic allocation benchmarks. Tactical asset allocation was a detractor, but manager selection contributed.

Tactical asset allocation had a negative impact on performance during the period. Trusts’ tactical underweights in U.S. large cap and overweights to EM equities were the main detractor, as large U.S. stocks delivered another period of positive performance, helped by continued strong corporate earnings and despite sharply rising long-term interest rates. EM equities struggled due to geopolitical tensions, economic uncertainties in key regions and a stronger U.S. dollar.

Underlying managers’ relative results were positive across the Trusts. Strategies that contributed most to excess returns in the quarter were Wellington International Opportunities Fund, Voya Core Plus Fixed Income, Lazard International Quality Growth and BNYM Insight Global Core Plus. The biggest detractors in the quarter were Voya VACS Series EME, Brandywine Global US Fixed Income Trust Fund and Polaris Capital International Value.

Principal Risks: There is no guarantee that any investment option will achieve its stated objective. Principal value fluctuates and there is no guarantee of value at any time, including the target date. The target date is the approximate date when investors plan to start withdrawing their money. When their target date is achieved they may have more or less than the original amount invested. For each target-date portfolio, until the day prior to its target date, the Trust will seek to provide total return consistent with an asset allocation targeted at retirement in approximately each Trust’s designated target year. On the target date, the Trust’s investment objective will be to seek to provide a combination of total return and stability of principal consistent with an asset allocation targeted to retirement. Stocks are more volatile than bonds, and trusts with a higher concentration of stocks are more likely to experience greater fluctuations in value than portfolios with a higher concentration in bonds. Foreign stocks and small and midcap stocks may be more volatile than large cap stocks. Investing in bonds also entails credit risk and interest rate risk. Generally, investors with longer timeframes can consider assuming more risk in their investment portfolios. The Voya Target Solution Trusts are actively managed, and the asset allocation adjusted over time. The trusts may merge with or change to other trusts over time. Refer to the prospectus for more information about the specific risks of investing in the various assets classes included in the Voya Target Solution Trusts. As with any portfolio, you could lose money on your investment in the Voya Target Solution Trust. Although asset allocation seeks to optimize returns given various levels of risk tolerance, you still may lose money and experience volatility. Market and asset class performance may differ in the future from historical performance and the assumptions used to form the asset allocations for the Voya Target Solution Trust. There is a risk that you could achieve better returns in an underlying portfolio or other portfolios representing a single asset class than in the Voya Target Solution Trust. Important factors to consider when planning for retirement include your expected expenses, sources of income and available assets. Before investing in the Voya Target Solution Trust, weigh your objectives, time horizon and risk tolerance. The Voya Target Solutions Trust invests in many underlying portfolios, which are exposed to the risks of different areas of the market. The higher a portfolio’s allocation to stocks the greater the portfolio’s overall risk. Diversification cannot assure a profit or protect against loss in a declining market. This commentary has been prepared by Voya Investment Management for informational purposes. Nothing contained herein should be construed as (i) an offer to sell or solicitation of an offer to buy any security or (ii) a recommendation as to the advisability of investing in, purchasing or selling any security. Any opinions expressed herein reflect our judgment and are subject to change. Certain of the statements contained herein are statements of future expectations and other forward-looking statements that are based on management’s current views and assumptions and involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results, performance, or events to differ materially from those expressed or implied in such statements. Actual results, performance, or events may differ materially from those in such statements due to, without limitation, (1) general economic conditions, (2) performance of financial markets, (3) interest rate levels, (4) increasing levels of loan defaults (5) changes in laws and regulations and (6) changes in the policies of governments and/or regulatory authorities. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

The opinions, views, and information expressed in this commentary regarding holdings are subject to change without notice. The information provided regarding holdings is not a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Portfolio holdings are fluid and are subject to daily change based on market conditions and other factors. The Fund discussed may be available to you as part of your employer sponsored retirement plan. There may be additional plan level fees resulting in personal performance to vary from stated performance. Please call your benefits office for more information. Variable annuities and group annuities are long-term investments designed for retirement purposes. If withdrawals are taken prior to age 59½, an IRS 10% premature distribution penalty tax may apply. Money taken from the annuity will be taxed as ordinary income in the year the money is distributed. An annuity does not provide any additional tax deferral benefit, as tax deferral is provided by the plan. Annuities may be subject to additional fees and expenses to which other tax-qualified funding vehicles may not be subject. However, an annuity does provide other features and benefits, such as lifetime income payments and death benefits, which may be valuable to you. Variable investments, of any kind, are not guaranteed and are subject to investment risk including the possible loss of principal. The investment return and principal value of the security will fluctuate so that when redeemed, it may be worth more or less than the original investment. In addition, there is no guarantee that any variable investment option will meet its stated objective. All guarantees are based on the financial strength and claims paying ability of the issuing insurance company, who is solely responsible for all obligations under its policies. Insurance products, annuities, and funding agreements issued by Voya Retirement Insurance and Annuity Company (“VRIAC”), One Orange Way, Windsor, CT 06095, which is solely responsible for meeting its obligations. Plan administrative services provided by VRIAC or Voya Institutional Plan Services, LLC (“VIPS”). Securities distributed by or offered through Voya Financial Partners, LLC (“VFP”) (member SIPC)or other broker-dealers with which it has a selling agreement. Only Voya Retirement Insurance and Annuity Company is admitted and can issue products in the state of New York. All companies are members of Voya Financial.

data.title: Alaris: Tariffs Vs. Compelling Valuation For 8% Yielder
data.description:
Note that Alaris was trading at $16.86 CAD at the time of writing. All values are in CAD unless noted otherwise. The ownership of this stock is generally restricted to Canadians. If you are wondering "What Is That Aboot?" you can read more here.

We have generally had a positive outlook of Alaris Equity Partners Income Trust (TSX:AD.UN:CA). We saw it as a quality royalty player with minimal levels of leverage and a history of creating value. But screaming "buy" at every single time point, adds little value to the conversation. If you cannot have a single "sell" rating on a stock, then you don't understand investing. Everything is a "sell" at the right price and while Alaris did not make it all the way there, we saw the opportunity to downgrade it to a less optimistic rating.

The stock has retracted in the tariffs melee and moved a bit lower.

We look at what the tea leaves hold for our star player.

The Company

We have written about the basics several times before, but it is worth repeating that the company's preferred equity works similarly to a royalty structure. The company is one of the least likely to experience any form of margin pressures, though the underlying partners are a different story.

Alaris has primarily top-line exposure and the distributions are reset annually based on some pre-determined parameters. These are customized on a deal-by-deal basis. The company generally has "collars" on the distribution changes. You can see that below in the distribution collar line.

So this gives Alaris some inflation protection over time.

So that was the base business, but the company has also been getting some extra action on the side. Alaris has also expanded its managed assets and the recent transaction has been a big boost to their AUM.

While the company is small, and it has had a few missteps, the overall performance has been fairly impressive. We like how they break down every single exit here, something that you will never find in the US BDC presentations.

What you usually find is the insistence that they have very few non-accruals, primarily because they have sold all their poorly performing loans.

Current Setup

Alaris gives you the chance to invest in a nice variety of investments paying a very high royalty payment to the company (and indirectly to you).

The company's recently reported results show it firing on all cylinders with the all-important net distributable cash flow increasing to $2.87 per unit.

The increase in book value is also fairly impressive, though, to some extent, that is reflective of the low credit spreads at the time (December 31, 2024). The partner distribution coverage remains fairly very strong and even one of the yellow flags present in Q3-2024 has disappeared.

Overall the company looks like one you might want to buy, even at a substantial premium.

Outlook & Verdict

Alaris is one of those strange Canadian companies that seems to invest primarily on the other side of the border. 90% of the fair value of its underlying holdings comes from the US. While it is well diversified by sectors, one has to take into account the implications in today's geopolitics.

The implications here are that a US recession is far more probable today than it was a month back. All the businesses Alaris has invested in will be impacted, the only question remains, to what extent. For businesses importing from China and other Asian countries, the impacts could be fairly substantial. We have low visibility into this and this is likely to be brought up at the next conference call in May.

We also look at the fundamentals of the currency exchange. This has worked wonderfully for Alaris over the last 12 months as the Canadian dollar has been extremely weak relative to the US Dollar. But we think this is about to reverse, and the trend is coming to an end. There are several fundamental reasons for this including what is needed for tariffs to really work, a weaker US Dollar that the administration will orchestrate. Alaris will feel the impact on its NAV and on its cash flow as it translates into a smaller amount of Canadian dollars. The company has very modest currency hedges in place.

Alaris has total contracts to sell US$30.25 million forward at an average of $1.39 CAD. For the following twelve months, Alaris has total contracts to sell US$9.5 million forward at an average of $1.41 CAD.

Source: Alaris Q4-2024 Financials.

So it should more or less feel the full impact of this, if the currency pair trades where we expect it to.

The good part here, is that the valuation is fairly solid. At a 30% discount to tangible book, you just need to dodge a severe recession, and you will do just fine.

Alaris' valuation contrasts with the top BDC which still trades at a legendary 55% premium to book value.

We think Alaris makes sense here as part of a diversified portfolio. We are raising our rating once again to Buy and think the odds of 10% annual returns from here are fairly high.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lakeland Industries, Inc. 2025 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Lakeland Industries, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Trump's 'Sledgehammer' Tariff Reversal - Our Reaction
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Padhraic Garvey, Chris Turner, & James Knightley

Carsten Brzeski: 'Caution is warranted'

US President Trump has decided on a 90-day reprieve for the "higher reciprocal tariffs" for countries that have not "retaliated" against his announcements from last week. That is, all countries except China. This means every country is now at the "universal" tariff rate of 10% for the next 90 days. China, though, because it put tariffs on US-made products entering China to 84%, will now receive a 125% tariff.

Apparently, this shift wasn't because of the financial market carnage we have been seeing, but Trump declaring "a victory" - the markets apparently did not understand Trump's policies, according to Treasury Secretary Bessent. We have our own interpretation.

Given that next to Mexico and Canada, the most important trading partners of the US, China and the EU, have not gone into any negotiations, makes this ‘victory’ look like celebrating a big party when your favorite national soccer team just won against San Marino. No offence to San Marino, but you know what I mean.

Earlier today, European member states agreed on a proposal by the European Commission to impose retaliatory measures on US imports from 15 April onwards, with a second set of measures following on 15 May. These measures would mainly target specific products, not entire sectors, with soybeans being the main target. With Donald Trump’s announcement to pause US tariffs, the EU is unlikely to implement its own announced measures. While this might bring some short-term relief for European exporters, the harm to confidence will last.

Let’s not forget that some 15% of the US’s imports come from China, and a big chunk more comes from China via Vietnam, Thailand, Malaysia and other Asian countries, writes James Knightley. The announced tariffs on Chinese products could give a total tariff revenue of $835bn. Unless China simply stops exporting. Something European corporates had already announced in recent days. Without exports, there are no tariff revenues.

In all honesty, the current situation is not only chaotic, it’s crazy, says Brzeski. Extrapolating short-term trends and events into the future has turned out to be a very risky strategy.

For the time being, Trump’s announcement has led to a rally on stock markets and will probably continue on Thursday when Asian and European markets open. However, don’t forget that we have been here before with announcements and then we get some pauses, only for the originally announced tariff to be reintroduced again. As such caution remains warranted - and remember President Trump does need revenue to fund his promised tax cuts. It would be a surprise if tonight’s announcement was really the return of ‘common sense’.

Padhraic Garvey: US Treasuries give the pause trade a mild thumbs up

Risk is back on, post the pause news, with material rallies on equity markets (the ones open), and some retightening in credit spreads (high yield 5yr CDX is tighter by a notable 60bp). That makes a whole lot of sense, based on the theory that the general sense of angst has been pared back. Market rates, though, are less excited, which is a reminder that there remains a material elevation of angst. That makes sense too, as the tariff wall remains; it’s just been toned down in places, and for just 90 days.

Front-end market rates were initially up by 20bp, but have since been pared back by 5bp. So still up, but not by enough to mark a material change in overall perception. There's even less reaction on the 10yr yield, which now just feels comfortable up in the 4.4% area, it seems. Bonds are essentially telegraphing that the pause is meaningful, but at the same time, not a whole lot has changed. It will, in a sense, if the 90 days become more permanent. But until then, there remains a US bond market that is coming out of this first tariff sortie a tad badly tainted by the whole thing.

Chris Turner: Trump turns the FX market upside down

No sooner than we started to have a clear view of what a global trade war would mean for the global FX market, Trump turned that view on its head. In response to the 90-day pause announcement, the defensive Japanese yen and Swiss franc have been sold heavily (USD/JPY has rallied nearly 3% off its intra-day low), while those EM and commodity-linked currencies in the firing line of a global trade war have come back strongly. Also helping the rotation away from defensive currencies has been a little stability in the US Treasury market, helped in part by a decent 10-year auction.

Whether the pause sticks remains to be seen, and what may also hold back a much larger recovery in commodity and EM currencies is what happens with China. The US and China are currently in a powerplay game of brinkmanship. Until a deal is announced or a big, bilateral meeting confirmed, USD/CNY will now be the focal attention of the FX market. This pause might bring some temporary relief to an FX market suffering from high volatility, and today is starting to show some strains in terms of liquidity. However, second-guessing the President’s next move has been a painful process to many, and ultimately we think he will get his way with a broadly weaker dollar, albeit a story for later this year and into 2026.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Trump Yields To Bond Market
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump does U-turn on tariffs, pausing on nearly all countries, but raising China rate. (0:16) Stocks explode higher, with Dow up nearly 3,000 points. (2:50) Bond yield rise forced Trump’s hand. (3:11)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, you may not be able to fight the Fed. And you certainly can’t beat the bond market.

In a massive U-turn, President Donald Trump let most countries off the hook from the large tariffs he announced last week, instead zeroing in on an escalating battle with China.

And stocks are rocketing higher, with the S&P 500 (SP500) up about +8%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP.IND) up +10% and the Dow (DJI) up +7%.

Mohamed El-Erian, adviser at Allianz, says: “Up to an hour ago, there was a debate on what would convince the US Administration to opt for some type of pause on tariffs.”

“Would it be Congress, the President's advisors, business leaders, the legal system, markets, or something else? We got the answer today: It's the government bond market--particularly, how close it gets to the line that separates wild price volatility from market malfunctioning.”

Facing a big selloff in stocks and (more importantly at this point) a persistently big rise in Treasury yields when part of the plan was to get rates down, the White House went from declaring no exemptions, no pauses and negotiations one country at a time to eliminate every trade deficit to a 90-day pause with tariffs dropping to 10% on the majority of the countries on the original list.

But Trump also boosted tariffs on China again, in response to an earlier move by Beijing.

"Based on the lack of respect that China has shown to the World’s Markets, I am hereby raising the Tariff charged to China by the United States of America to 125%, effective immediately," Trump said on Truth Social.

The president said other countries had called U.S. federal agencies such as the departments of Commerce and Treasury to "negotiate a solution to the subjects being discussed relative to Trade, Trade Barriers, Tariffs, Currency Manipulation, and Non-Monetary Tariffs."

"These Countries have not, at my strong suggestion, retaliated in any way, shape, or form against the United States," he said.

"I have authorized a 90-day PAUSE, and a substantially lowered Reciprocal Tariff during this period, of 10%, also effective immediately," he added.

Earlier on Wednesday, Beijing retaliated against U.S. tariffs of 104% by raising its tariff rate on imports from the U.S. to 84%. The world's second-largest economy called the American trade barriers "a mistake on top of a mistake."

There is some vagary in what countries are included in the pause.

Robin Brooks, senior fellow at the Brookings Institute, says: “This trade war was really always about the US vs China. The US made a strategic error by picking fights with everyone, instead of just focusing on China. The 90 day pause fixes that and is thus very good. It also gives Xi an off-ramp if he reverses China's retaliatory tariffs.”

In trading, the bulls charged immediately.

Among the biggest gainers in the S&P 500 were airlines and chip stocks, up more than 20%. Sherwood News notes that Broadcom (AVGO), Nvdia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Super Micro (SMCI) and Palantir (PLTR) are recouping all of their post-tariff losses.

Dollar stores, supermarkets, fast food chains and dividend plays struggled.

But bond trading is still not following the script. Treasury yields are off their highs but still elevated. The 10-year yield (US10Y), which had neared 4.5% overnight, is still above 4.4%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) is near 4%.

So, having been burned before, traders may be wary about whether new tariffs could be just around the corner.

Right before Trump’s announcement, The Treasury Department's auction of $39 billion 10-year notes drew surprisingly strong demand.

The auction's high yield of 4.435% came in significantly below the when-issued market yield (also known as the snap price) of 4.465%. That denoted a trade through of 3 basis points, which is tied for the second-best on record.

Wedbush analyst Dan Ives says: “The pressure on Trump took on a life of its own, and the eye-popping rise of the 10-year yield was ultimately too much to hold his line on the self-inflicted tariff slate unleashed at midnight. Now expect massive negotiations across the board - China the wild card for US tech sector.”

Kathy Jones, chief fixed income strategist at Schwab, simply said: “Bonds rule.”
